mnemonics.title: Read Rule Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: READ IF: TS(T) ≥ WTS(Q) - Think: Your Start Time must be Newer than the Last Writer. If Older (TS < WTS), you're too late - ABORT!

mnemonics.title: Write Rule Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: WRITE IF: TS(T) ≥ RTS(Q) AND TS(T) ≥ WTS(Q) - Think: You must be Newer than both the Last Reader AND Last Writer. Two NEWs to WRITE!

mnemonics.title: Thomas' Rule Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: Thomas' Write: If TS < WTS → IGNORE (someone newer wrote). If TS < RTS → ROLLBACK (someone newer read). Remember: W=Ignore, R=Rollback!

mnemonics.title: TSO Advantages
mnemonics.content: No Deadlock (D), No Cascade (C), Simple (S) - Remember: DCS - Don't Cause Serializability problems!

acronyms.usage: A concurrency control protocol that uses transaction timestamps to determine serializability order
The largest timestamp of any transaction that has successfully read a data item
The largest timestamp of any transaction that has successfully written a data item
Same as TSO - the protocol name
A lock-based concurrency control protocol for comparison
Techniques to ensure correct concurrent execution of transactions

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a timeline with timestamps. For a transaction to read, its timestamp must be to the RIGHT of the last write timestamp on the timeline. If it's to the LEFT, the data is too new for you - ABORT!
Two barriers: Read Barrier (RTS) and Write Barrier (WTS). Your timestamp must jump over BOTH barriers to write. If either is higher, you cannot pass - ROLLBACK!
A fork in the road: If your timestamp < WTS (someone newer wrote), take the IGNORE path (continue). If your timestamp < RTS (someone newer read), take the ROLLBACK path (abort).
Use a number line: Younger transactions have HIGHER timestamps. Think of timestamps as priority - Higher number = More Recent = Higher Priority!
Each transaction gets a ticket with a number at the service counter. Higher number = arrived later but gets served later (in timestamp order, earlier timestamp = served first).

analogies.analogy: Like a movie theater booking system where tickets are issued with sequential numbers. The earlier ticket (lower timestamp) gets to choose seats first, and later tickets must accept the remaining seats or leave (abort).
Think of a shared Google Doc with 'Last Modified by' and 'Last Viewed by' timestamps. Before editing, you check if anyone viewed/modified it after you started. If yes, your version is stale!

In TO, each transaction works in an isolated bubble - they don't see each other's uncommitted work. Like each person having their own copy of a document to edit, never touching the original until commit.

Like a collaborative whiteboard where if someone draws something after you, but you were looking at an old version, you just ignore your old drawing rather than erasing the new one - it's already obsolete!

analogies.analization: When a transaction aborts in TO, it's like being given a new ticket with a higher number and going back to the end of the line - you get another chance with updated information.
